summary: Gruber dissects how a reasonable KGI analyst report about the iPhone X potentially being discontinued after one year got distorted into sensationalist headlines claiming Apple was 'cancelling' the iPhone X due to poor sales. He notes this wouldn't even be unprecedented, pointing to the iPhone 5 being replaced by the 5S/5C without lingering in the lineup. The real reason, Gruber argues, is likely manufacturing complexity and cost (especially OLED displays), not disappointing demand. He systematically dismantles the Newsweek and Forbes articles for drawing conclusions not present in the original source, particularly the false implication that Apple would pull the iPhone X before its successor ships. The piece is ultimately about how the modern media ecosystem incentivizes misleading headlines over accurate reporting.
key_points: The iPhone X being produced for only one year would not be unprecedented — the iPhone 5 and original iPhone were both dropped when successors launched, and radically new iPhone form factors typically don't stick around at lower prices
The likely reason for discontinuing the iPhone X is manufacturing difficulty and cost (particularly OLED displays), not poor sales — the same logic that led to the iPhone 5C replacing the iPhone 5
Newsweek's article fabricated a scenario where Apple stops selling the iPhone X months before replacements ship, a conclusion not present in the original KGI analyst report
iPhone X sales figures must be contextualized against the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus, which in any previous year would have been the flagship models themselves
The distortion chain from analyst note to AppleInsider summary to Newsweek clickbait exemplifies how modern media prioritizes social media traffic over reader trust
money_quotes: Throw in some poor reading comprehension skills and sensationalist mendacity, though, and you get Anthony Cuthbertson's report for the publication that today operates under the once-respected name Newsweek.
Cuthbertson's only source is AppleInsider's summary of Ming-Chi Kuo's report — and he's drawing conclusions that simply weren't in the original.
Kuo's record is only good regarding what is going on in Apple's Asian supply chain, not why.
If Kuo is correct that the iPhone X will be dropped from next year's iPhone lineup, I don't think the reason has anything to do with how well it sold.

Everything about Cuthbertson's report — starting with that fucking headline, which arguably is all that matters for a publication like today's Newsweek, which is, I would bet, hopelessly dependent upon social media traffic and utterly disdainful of the notion of building up a wellspring of trust with a devoted readership — seems written to paint a picture of the iPhone X being a sales failure, facts be damned.
This piece isn't even up to Forbes's usual standard of at least being worth rolling up and using as a sex toy on short-fingered vulgarians.
The more I think about it, the more it feels like it's fair to say that the norm is for radically new iPhone form factors not to remain in the lineup at a lower price the next year.
key_insight: The iPhone X being produced for only one year is a routine product-line decision driven by manufacturing complexity, not a sign of failure — but the modern media ecosystem reliably distorts such nuance into sensationalist clickbait.
